skill.name: streaming-api-patterns
skill.description: Implement real-time data streaming with Server-Sent Events (SSE), WebSockets, and ReadableStream APIs. Master backpressure handling, reconnection strategies, and LLM streaming for 2025+ real-time applications.
skill.summary: Implement real-time data streaming with Server-Sent Events (SSE), WebSockets, and ReadableStream API...
skill.author: AI Agent Hub

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported external command and weak cryptography patterns, but review found those were false positives from Markdown code fences, inline code, metadata text, and checklist wording. The network patterns are expected educational examples for SSE, WebSocket, fetch, and documentation links. One low-severity template issue remains because an error message is interpolated directly into an SSE JSON event.

low_findings.title: Unsafe Error Message Interpolation in SSE Template
low_findings.description: The template inserts error.message directly into a JSON-formatted SSE event string. This can leak internal error details and can break the event payload if the message contains quotes or control characters.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line directly interpolates an exception message into a JSON string without JSON.stringify. The issue affects example template safety, but there is no evidence of malicious intent or data exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Documentation Syntax Misclassified as Execution or Weak Crypto
low_findings.description: The reported external command and weak cryptography findings are not real execution or cryptography risks. The cited locations are Markdown code fences, inline formatting, metadata text, and checklist wording, not runnable shell or weak crypto code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review confirmed the cited text is documentation or TypeScript examples. No shell invocation, Ruby execution, or cryptographic implementation is present at these locations.

low_findings.title: Expected Network API Examples
low_findings.description: The network findings are legitimate examples for a streaming API skill. They show EventSource, WebSocket, fetch, SSE headers, and public documentation links without hidden exfiltration behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network calls and URLs match the skill's stated streaming API purpose. No credential collection, suspicious domain, or unexpected outbound endpoint was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Raw Exception Text in Streamed JSON Event
dangerous_patterns.description: The SSE template builds an error event with raw exception text instead of serializing a structured safe error object.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is visible in the template and can create malformed JSON or disclose details. It is a copyable example issue, not confirmed malicious behavior.

content.user_title: Build Streaming APIs With SSE and WebSockets
content.value_statement: Real-time features often fail because streaming protocols, reconnection, and backpressure are handled inconsistently. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical patterns for SSE, WebSockets, ReadableStream, and LLM token streaming.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to choose SSE, WebSockets, or ReadableStream for real-time application flows.
Provides TypeScript patterns for Next.js streaming route handlers and browser clients.
Covers LLM token streaming with completion markers and client-side stream parsing.
Describes reconnection, keepalive, heartbeat, and exponential backoff strategies.
Includes a checklist for security, performance, testing, and production readiness.
limitations: Examples are educational and need adaptation before use in production systems.
It does not provide a complete authentication or authorization framework.
It does not benchmark specific hosting providers or infrastructure limits.
The included SSE template should sanitize error output before production use.

use_cases.title: Stream LLM Responses
use_cases.description: Build chat interfaces that show model output as tokens arrive, with completion markers and client parsing.

use_cases.title: Add Live Product Updates
use_cases.description: Choose SSE or WebSockets for notifications, dashboards, progress updates, and collaborative application events.

use_cases.title: Review Streaming Readiness
use_cases.description: Use the checklist to verify reconnection, backpressure, rate limits, testing, and memory behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Streaming Protocol
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose between Server-Sent Events, WebSockets, and ReadableStream for my feature. Ask about direction, scale, authentication, and browser support.

prompt_templates.title: Design an SSE Endpoint
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a production-ready SSE endpoint for a Next.js application. Include headers, keepalive behavior, disconnect handling, and client parsing steps.

prompt_templates.title: Improve LLM Token Streaming
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my LLM streaming flow for latency, partial chunks, completion handling, error events, and user cancellation.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Real-Time Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit my real-time architecture for backpressure, reconnect storms, rate limits, message validation, observability, and long-running connection limits.

output_examples.input: I need live progress updates for a long-running export.
output_examples.output: Recommendation: use SSE because updates only travel from server to browser. Include keepalive events, abort handling, and a final completion event.

output_examples.input: My chat app needs users to send and receive messages instantly.
output_examples.output: Recommendation: use WebSockets because communication is bidirectional. Add authentication, origin checks, heartbeat, message size limits, and reconnect behavior.

output_examples.input: My LLM stream sometimes breaks during JSON parsing.
output_examples.output: Review chunk boundaries, partial Unicode handling, line buffering, completion markers, and safe error events before parsing each message.

best_practices: Match the protocol to message direction, connection count, and browser support.
Validate messages, authenticate connections, and rate limit real-time endpoints.
Test slow networks, reconnect paths, concurrent clients, and long-lived memory behavior.
anti_patterns: Using WebSockets for one-way updates when SSE would be simpler.
Streaming raw internal errors or unsanitized user content to clients.
Ignoring backpressure, browser connection limits, and reconnect storm behavior.

faq.question: Does this skill execute network requests?
faq.answer: No. It documents streaming patterns and includes example code that users may adapt.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Is SSE better than WebSockets?
faq.answer: SSE is better for one-way server updates. WebSockets are better for bidirectional communication.

faq.question: Does it cover LLM streaming?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes patterns for streaming model output, parsing events, and handling completion markers.

faq.question: Can I copy the template directly to production?
faq.answer: No. Review authentication, error sanitization, rate limits, and infrastructure settings first.

faq.question: Does it include testing guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. The checklist covers slow networks, reconnection, concurrency, load testing, errors, and memory leaks.
